mSDataSet.OriginalText: Battle Passes

A new Battle Pass will be available each season.
By increasing the level of your Battle Pass, you’ll
be able to earn various rewards.

TextInfos.Text: A new Battle Pass will be available each season.
By increasing the level of your Battle Pass, you’ll
be able to earn various rewards.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
You can raise the level of a Battle Pass by taking part in
Ranked Battles and competitions to obtain Season Points.

TextInfos.Text: You can raise the level of a Battle Pass by taking part in
Ranked Battles and competitions to obtain Season Points.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Some rewards are only available by purchasing a Premium
Battle Pass. The Premium Battle Pass can be purchased from
the Shop screen.

TextInfos.Text: Some rewards are only available by purchasing a Premium
Battle Pass. The Premium Battle Pass can be purchased from

mSDataSet.OriginalText: The Shop Screen

The Shop screen is divided into two sections:
the Premium Shop and the Frontier Shop.

TextInfos.Text: The Shop screen is divided into two sections:
the Premium Shop and the Frontier Shop.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
In the Premium Shop, you can purchase memberships,
Premium Battle Passes, and more.

TextInfos.Text: In the Premium Shop, you can purchase memberships,

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
In the Frontier Shop, you can exchange your VP for
things like held items for your Pokémon or new clothes.

TextInfos.Text: In the Frontier Shop, you can exchange your VP for
things like held items for your Pokémon or new clothes.

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Cordy’s Missions

With Cordy’s missions, you can earn and claim rewards by
fulfilling certain conditions. Some missions are updated on a
daily basis, while others are newly assigned each week.

TextInfos.Text: With Cordy’s missions, you can earn and claim rewards by
fulfilling certain conditions. Some missions are updated on a
daily basis, while others are newly assigned each week.

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Achievements

Here you can view various achievements you might set your
sights on completing. Meet their conditions and you’ll receive
rewards including badges, titles, and more!

TextInfos.Text: Here you can view various achievements you might set your
sights on completing. Meet their conditions and you’ll receive
rewards including badges, titles, and more!

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Online Competitions

Online Competitions allow you to battle other Trainers
around the world by connecting to the internet.

TextInfos.Text: Online Competitions allow you to battle other Trainers
around the world by connecting to the internet.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Enter a competition, and you’ll be able to battle your fellow
competitors for the length of the event. Some competitions
also offer special rewards. But note that if you ever need to
withdraw from a competition, you must do so before it begins!

TextInfos.Text: Enter a competition, and you’ll be able to battle your fellow
competitors for the length of the event. Some competitions
also offer special rewards. But note that if you ever need to
withdraw from a competition, you must do so before it begins!

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Battle Data

With the Battle Data feature, you can check the results
of various competitions and Ranked Battles seasons.
You can also see data about the Pokémon that were
most frequently used in battles.

TextInfos.Text: With the Battle Data feature, you can check the results
of various competitions and Ranked Battles seasons.
You can also see data about the Pokémon that were

mSDataSet.OriginalText: The Style Screen

In this area, you can change your clothing,
your hair and cosmetics, the pose you strike
when entering battle, and more.

TextInfos.Text: In this area, you can change your clothing,
your hair and cosmetics, the pose you strike

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Put together a combination that’s all your own,
then save it as a preset style!

TextInfos.Text: Put together a combination that’s all your own,
then save it as a preset style!

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Trainer Profiles

On the Profile screen, you can check a Trainer’s details, such as
their Trainer ID. When viewing your own profile, you can also
change up your badges, Trainer icon, and title.

TextInfos.Text: On the Profile screen, you can check a Trainer’s details, such as
their Trainer ID. When viewing your own profile, you can also
change up your badges, Trainer icon, and title.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
You can choose who can see your profile
in the Settings menu.

TextInfos.Text: You can choose who can see your profile

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Private Battles

Private Battles are held by creating rooms, in which you face
off against friends and other Trainers under various battle rules.

TextInfos.Text: Private Battles are held by creating rooms, in which you face
off against friends and other Trainers under various battle rules.

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Trainers can join a room by entering the correct Room ID.

TextInfos.Text: Trainers can join a room by entering the correct Room ID.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
If you’d rather spectate than battle, you can choose to watch
Private Battles taking place between other Trainers in the room.

TextInfos.Text: If you’d rather spectate than battle, you can choose to watch
Private Battles taking place between other Trainers in the room.

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Stats & More

On this screen you can see a summary of information about a
Pokémon and also set its battle name, title, and more.

TextInfos.Text: On this screen you can see a summary of information about a
Pokémon and also set its battle name, title, and more.

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Recruiting Pokémon

You can recruit Pokémon from the available
rosters you’ll find at Roster Ranch.

Every 22 hours, you’ll be able to meet a new lineup of
Pokémon to recruit from—without using VP! You can
shorten this waiting time using VP or Quick Coupons.

TextInfos.Text: Every 22 hours, you’ll be able to meet a new lineup of
Pokémon to recruit from—without using VP! You can
shorten this waiting time using VP or Quick Coupons.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Note: The lineup of Pokémon you can meet
during the tutorial is fixed and will not change.

TextInfos.Text: Note: The lineup of Pokémon you can meet
during the tutorial is fixed and will not change.

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Let’s get to recruitin’, shall we?

TextInfos.Text: Let’s get to recruitin’, shall we?

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Go on, now, don’t be shy! Pick out a Pokémon
that strikes your fancy.▼
And don’t forget—it’d usually cost you VP to go
recruitin’ a Pokémon permanently.▼
But just this once, I’ll let you pick for nothin’!

TextInfos.Text: Go on, now, don’t be shy! Pick out a Pokémon
And don’t forget—it’d usually cost you VP to go
But just this once, I’ll let you pick for nothin’!

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Training Pokémon

By using VP, you can train Pokémon to change their
stat points, stat alignments, moves, Abilities, and more.

TextInfos.Text: By using VP, you can train Pokémon to change their
stat points, stat alignments, moves, Abilities, and more.

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Replica Teams

With the Replica Team feature, you can make new
Battle Teams based on the teams that other Trainers
have shared. You can share your own teams, too!

TextInfos.Text: With the Replica Team feature, you can make new
Battle Teams based on the teams that other Trainers
have shared. You can share your own teams, too!

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
If your Pokémon have different stats, moves, or Abilities
than those on the Battle Team you’re replicating, you can
train all your Pokémon at once to make them match.

TextInfos.Text: If your Pokémon have different stats, moves, or Abilities
than those on the Battle Team you’re replicating, you can
train all your Pokémon at once to make them match.

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Collecting Pokémon from Pokémon HOME

Pokémon that you send from Pokémon HOME to
visit here in Pokémon Champions can be collected
from Roster Ranch.

TextInfos.Text: Pokémon that you send from Pokémon HOME to
visit here in Pokémon Champions can be collected

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
These visiting Pokémon may have their stats or Abilities
changed to help them be better suited to battle here.

TextInfos.Text: These visiting Pokémon may have their stats or Abilities
changed to help them be better suited to battle here.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Their moves won’t be changed automatically, but you can
always change them yourself by training your Pokémon.
This lets you replace any moves that can’t be used here
in Pokémon Champions.

TextInfos.Text: Their moves won’t be changed automatically, but you can
always change them yourself by training your Pokémon.
This lets you replace any moves that can’t be used here

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Sending Pokémon Back to Pokémon HOME

TextInfos.Text: Sending Pokémon Back to Pokémon HOME

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Pokémon you’ve brought over to visit from Pokémon HOME
can be sent back to Pokémon HOME at any time.

TextInfos.Text: Pokémon you’ve brought over to visit from Pokémon HOME
can be sent back to Pokémon HOME at any time.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Training done here won’t carry over to Pokémon HOME, but if
the same Pokémon visit Pokémon Champions again, any training
they received here will be saved as long as those Pokémon are
still in the same form as when they last visited.

TextInfos.Text: Training done here won’t carry over to Pokémon HOME, but if
the same Pokémon visit Pokémon Champions again, any training
they received here will be saved as long as those Pokémon are
still in the same form as when they last visited.

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Your Pokémon Box

In your Box, you can view the Pokémon you’ve
recruited and make yourself some Battle Teams.

TextInfos.Text: In your Box, you can view the Pokémon you’ve
recruited and make yourself some Battle Teams.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Use the sorting and filtering functions, or choose
to label or favorite certain Pokémon, to make them
easy to find whenever you want.

TextInfos.Text: Use the sorting and filtering functions, or choose
to label or favorite certain Pokémon, to make them
easy to find whenever you want.

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Ranked Battles

In Ranked Battles, you can face off against other Trainers from
around the world in the Single Battle or Double Battle format.
Each season is a new chance to compete!

TextInfos.Text: In Ranked Battles, you can face off against other Trainers from
around the world in the Single Battle or Double Battle format.
Each season is a new chance to compete!

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
You’ll move up or down through the ranks based on the
number of wins and losses you accumulate. If you reach the
Master Ball Tier, you’ll then compete with your fellow Trainers
for your final placement, which is based on your rating.

TextInfos.Text: You’ll move up or down through the ranks based on the
number of wins and losses you accumulate. If you reach the
Master Ball Tier, you’ll then compete with your fellow Trainers
for your final placement, which is based on your rating.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
When each battle season ends, you’ll be able to receive
rewards depending on the final rank you reached or how
high you managed to place.

TextInfos.Text: When each battle season ends, you’ll be able to receive
rewards depending on the final rank you reached or how

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Once a new season starts, all results will be reset and
every Trainer will begin once more from the Poké Ball Tier.

TextInfos.Text: Once a new season starts, all results will be reset and
every Trainer will begin once more from the Poké Ball Tier.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Check the News for more information about the regulations
for each season.

TextInfos.Text: Check the News for more information about the regulations

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Casual Battles

In Casual Battles, you can face off against other Trainers from
around the world in the Single Battle or Double Battle format.
No need to worry about winning or losing—these battles
won’t affect your current rank!

TextInfos.Text: In Casual Battles, you can face off against other Trainers from
around the world in the Single Battle or Double Battle format.
No need to worry about winning or losing—these battles

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Single Battles

In Single Battles, you’ll select three Pokémon from your
Battle Team, sending them out one at a time to do battle.
Victory will go to the Trainer who first defeats all of their
opponent’s Pokémon.

TextInfos.Text: Victory will go to the Trainer who first defeats all of their

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Select Fight in the lower right-hand corner of the
screen to be taken to the move selection screen.

TextInfos.Text: in the lower right-hand corner of the
screen to be taken to the

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Once you’ve reached the move selection screen,
select Body Slam from your Pokémon’s available moves.
It’s time to dish out an attack on your opponent!

TextInfos.Text: It’s time to dish out an attack on your opponent!

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Once you’ve reached the move selection screen,
tap Body Slam from your Pokémon’s available moves.
Tap it a second time to finalize your selection and
dish out an attack on your opponent!

TextInfos.Text: Tap it a second time to finalize your selection and
dish out an attack on your opponent!

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
In a Pokémon battle, the Pokémon on both sides
will unleash moves just like this, one after another.
Try to defeat the opposing Trainer by reducing the HP
of all their Pokémon to zero!

TextInfos.Text: In a Pokémon battle, the Pokémon on both sides
will unleash moves just like this, one after another.
Try to defeat the opposing Trainer by
of all their Pokémon to zero

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Next up, let’s take a look at type matchups, which
are critical for getting the upper hand in a battle.
Every Pokémon and all the moves they use
have at least one type associated with them.

TextInfos.Text: Next up, let’s take a look at
are critical for getting the upper hand in a battle.
Every Pokémon and all the moves they use

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
A Grass-type Pokémon will take double the
damage if hit with a Fire-type move.
But a Fire-type Pokémon will take just half
the damage if hit with a Grass-type move.

TextInfos.Text: the damage if hit with a

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
These differences in the effectiveness of moves is how
type matchups play a role in Pokémon battles.

TextInfos.Text: These differences in the effectiveness of moves is how
type matchups play a role in Pokémon battles.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
The Venusaur you’re up against has the Grass type.
Try using a Fire-type move against it, now that you know
how effective Fire-type moves are against Grass types.

Select Fight, then go for Flamethrower!

TextInfos.Text: The Venusaur you’re up against has the Grass type.
Try using a Fire-type move against it, now that you know
how effective Fire-type moves are against Grass types.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
You defeated the Grass-type Venusaur using
the Fire-type move Flamethrower!

Use type matchups to choose the most effective
moves possible and take control of your battles.

TextInfos.Text: You defeated the Grass-type Venusaur using
Use type matchups to choose the most effective
moves possible and take control of your battles.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
When a Pokémon faints, a Trainer must then
switch in another Pokémon from their party.

Your next opponent appears to be the Water-type
Pokémon Blastoise.

Water-type moves deal double damage against Fire-type
Pokémon, so your side is now at a disadvantage.

TextInfos.Text: Your next opponent appears to be the Water-type
Water-type moves deal double damage against Fire-type
Pokémon, so your side is now at a disadvantage.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
When you find yourself on the wrong end of an unfavorable
type matchup, switching your Pokémon out so you can send in
a different Pokémon to fight can be a good strategy.
Let’s give it a try. Start by selecting Pokémon.

TextInfos.Text: When you find yourself on the wrong end of an unfavorable
type matchup, switching your Pokémon out so you can send in
a different Pokémon to fight can be a good strategy.
Let’s give it a try. Start by selecting

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
When you select Pokémon, you’ll be taken to another screen,
where you can choose a different Pokémon to switch in.
Why don’t you select Meganium and send it into this battle?

TextInfos.Text: you’ll be taken to another screen,
where you can choose a different Pokémon to switch in.
and send it into this battle?

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
You switched in Meganium, which has a type advantage against
Blastoise. Pokémon can’t use moves on the turn they switch in,
but since damage from Water-type moves is halved against
Grass types, you were able to limit the damage you took.

TextInfos.Text: You switched in Meganium, which has a type advantage against
Blastoise. Pokémon can’t use moves on the turn they switch in,
but since damage from Water-type moves is halved against
Grass types, you were able to limit the damage you took.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Now let’s try having Meganium use Bullet Seed, a Grass-type
move that will deal double damage against Water types.

TextInfos.Text: Now let’s try having Meganium use
move that will deal double damage against Water types.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
You defeated Blastoise!

Remember that switching in different Pokémon during
a battle can be an effective tool for seizing victory.

TextInfos.Text: Remember that switching in different Pokémon during
a battle can be an effective tool for seizing victory.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Your opponent has now sent out Heracross.

Try using the Psychic-type move Zen Headbutt, which
deals double damage against Fighting-type Pokémon.

TextInfos.Text: Your opponent has now sent out

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Whichever Pokémon has the higher Speed stat
will usually get to move first each turn.

Your opponent’s Heracross will be the first one to attack
because it has a higher Speed stat than your Meganium.

TextInfos.Text: will usually get to move first each turn.
Your opponent’s Heracross will be the first one to attack
because it has a higher Speed stat than your Meganium.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
In that case, why don’t you try switching in a
speedier Pokémon so you can attack first?

Go ahead and switch in Pidgeot, which has a
higher Speed stat than Heracross.

TextInfos.Text: In that case, why don’t you try switching in a
speedier Pokémon so you can attack first?

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Pidgeot is now in the battle, and since it has a
higher Speed stat than the opposing Heracross,
it should be able to attack first.

Try having Pidgeot use its Hurricane move!

TextInfos.Text: Pidgeot is now in the battle, and since it has a
it should be able to attack first.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Pidgeot’s Speed stat was higher than Heracross’s,
so it was able to attack first and knock Heracross out!

Since you managed to defeat all three Pokémon
from the other side, that means you win this battle!

Now, keep it up and have fun battling Pokémon!

TextInfos.Text: Pidgeot’s Speed stat was higher than Heracross’s,
so it was able to attack first and knock Heracross out!
Since you managed to defeat all
from the other side, that means you win this battle!
Now, keep it up and have fun battling Pokémon!

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Double Battles

In Double Battles, you and an opponent will each select
four Pokémon from your Battle Teams, sending them out
two at a time to do battle. Victory will go to the Trainer
who first defeats all of their opponent’s Pokémon.

TextInfos.Text: , you and an opponent will each select
from your Battle Teams, sending them out
to do battle. Victory will go to the Trainer
who first defeats all of their opponent’s Pokémon.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
These battles allow you to enjoy more challenging strategies.
You’ll need to remember the basics that apply to Single Battles
while also making new decisions, like which of your
opponent’s two Pokémon to target with your attacks.

TextInfos.Text: These battles allow you to enjoy more challenging strategies.
You’ll need to remember the basics that apply to Single Battles
while also making new decisions, like which of your
opponent’s two Pokémon to target with your attacks.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
In Double Battles, you get to choose the actions for
two different Pokémon from your team each turn. First,
we’ll start with Primarina. Select Fight, then have
Primarina use its move Moonblast.

TextInfos.Text: In Double Battles, you get to choose
two different Pokémon from your team each turn

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Since this is a Double Battle, you’ll need to choose
which Pokémon to target after selecting a move.
In this case, go ahead and choose Snorlax.

TextInfos.Text: Since this is a Double Battle, you’ll need to choose
In this case, go ahead and choose

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Now let’s select an action for Garchomp to take.

This time, how about using Rock Slide to attack both of
your opponent’s Pokémon at once?

Select Fight for your Garchomp and go for Rock Slide.
Then choose both of the opposing Pokémon as the targets.

TextInfos.Text: Now let’s select an action for
for your Garchomp and go for Rock Slide.
Then choose both of the opposing Pokémon as the targets.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
With Garchomp’s Rock Slide, you were able to attack
both of the opposing Pokémon at once.

You can sometimes seize the advantage in Double Battles by
using multitarget moves like this, so keep this strategy in mind!

Note: Multitarget moves will deal less damage when used
against two targets than when they’re used against one target,
so make sure you take this into account.

TextInfos.Text: With Garchomp’s Rock Slide, you were able to attack
both of the opposing Pokémon at once.
You can sometimes seize the advantage in Double Battles by
using multitarget moves like this, so keep this strategy in mind!
Note: Multitarget moves will deal less damage when used
against two targets than when they’re used against one target,
so make sure you take this into account.

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Mega Evolution

Mega Evolution is a mechanic only available to certain
Pokémon. It changes a Pokémon’s appearance, raises
its overall stats, and has the potential to greatly expand
the strategies you employ in battle.

TextInfos.Text: is a mechanic only available to certain
Pokémon. It changes a Pokémon’s appearance, raises
its overall stats, and has the potential to greatly expand
the strategies you employ in battle.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
A Pokémon capable of Mega Evolution can Mega Evolve
if it’s holding a Mega Stone that’s specific to its species.

Your Charizard is a Pokémon that can Mega Evolve,
and it’s holding a Mega Stone: Charizardite X!

TextInfos.Text: Your Charizard is a Pokémon that can Mega Evolve,
and it’s holding a Mega Stone:

mSDataSet.OriginalText: To have it Mega Evolve, first select Fight.

TextInfos.Text: To have it Mega Evolve, first select

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Next, choose the Mega Evolve option, then select a move to
trigger your Pokémon’s Mega Evolution. If you change your
mind about Mega Evolving, you can also back out by choosing
the Mega Evolve option once more.

TextInfos.Text: Next, choose the Mega Evolve option, then
trigger your Pokémon’s Mega Evolution. If you change your
mind about Mega Evolving, you can also back out by choosing
the Mega Evolve option once more.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Now, why don’t you select the move Dragon Claw
after choosing the Mega Evolve option?

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Since Charizard Mega Evolved into Mega Charizard X
and had its stats raised, it was able to defeat Goodra!

Mega Evolution can only be used one time per battle,
so think carefully about which Pokémon you’ll want to
Mega Evolve and give them a Mega Stone to hold!

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Move Priority

The order in which Pokémon use their moves is usually
determined by which of them has the higher Speed stat.

Test this out by selecting Fight and then having Lucario
use its Meteor Mash move.

TextInfos.Text: The order in which Pokémon use their moves is usually
determined by which of them has the higher

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
The opposing Alakazam has a higher Speed stat,
so it was able to attack first.

However, there are some moves that allow the user
to act first even when facing speedier opponents.

These moves are described as having a high priority,
and they’re called priority moves. Moves with higher
priority are used before moves with lower priority.

TextInfos.Text: so it was able to attack first.
However, there are some moves that allow the user
to act first even when facing speedier opponents.
These moves are described as having a high
priority are used before moves with lower priority.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
For any priority move, you can check how high
its priority is by selecting Fight, then looking at
the relevant number in the Move Info window that
can be displayed on the move selection screen.

TextInfos.Text: For any priority move, you can check
by selecting Fight, then looking at
can be displayed on the move selection screen.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
To see for yourself what happens to the order of moves
in a battle when you use a priority move, try having your
Lucario use Bullet Punch this time.

TextInfos.Text: To see for yourself what happens to the order of moves
in a battle when you use a

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Ta-da! Your Lucario was able to attack first, even though
it was up against an opponent with a higher Speed stat.

Use priority moves wisely to help your team stay
one step ahead of the opposition!

TextInfos.Text: Ta-da! Your Lucario was able to attack first, even though
it was up against an opponent with a higher Speed stat.
Use priority moves wisely to help your team stay
one step ahead of the opposition!

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Physical vs. Special Moves

Attack moves can be either physical moves or special moves.
Physical moves will do more damage the higher the Attack stat
of the Pokémon using them. Special moves, on the other hand,
will do more damage the higher the Sp. Atk stat of the user.

TextInfos.Text: Physical moves will do more damage the higher the
of the Pokémon using them. Special moves, on the other hand,
will do more damage the higher the

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
But these moves aren’t impacted only by the user’s Attack
or Sp. Atk stat. Physical moves will also deal LESS damage
the higher the target’s Defense stat is. Likewise, special moves
will deal less damage if the target has a high Sp. Def stat.

TextInfos.Text: But these moves aren’t impacted only by the user’s Attack
or Sp. Atk stat. Physical moves will also deal LESS damage
will deal less damage if the target has a high

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
The Avalugg you’re facing off against has a high Defense
stat but a low Sp. Def stat.

As a test, try using Dragon Darts, which is a physical move.

TextInfos.Text: you’re facing off against has a

Since the target has a high Defense stat, the attack didn’t
do all that much damage.

Next, try using Dragon Pulse, which is a special move.

TextInfos.Text: Since the target has a high Defense stat, the attack didn’t

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Because the target has a low Sp. Def stat, your Pokémon’s
move was able to deal a lot of damage.

But remember that it’s not only the target’s Defense and
Sp. Def stats that matter!

Physical moves will deal more damage when used by a
Pokémon with a high Attack stat, while special moves will
deal more damage when used by a Pokémon with a high
Sp. Atk stat.

TextInfos.Text: Because the target has a low Sp. Def stat, your Pokémon’s

Take into account your Pokémon’s stats as well as

those of your opponent to determine whether a
situation calls for physical moves or special moves!

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Status Conditions

If you inflict an opponent with a status condition,
you can quickly turn a battle to your advantage.

To see this effect in action, have Arcanine use
the move Will-O-Wisp.

TextInfos.Text: If you inflict an opponent with a
you can quickly turn a battle to your advantage.
To see this effect in action, have

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
After the opponent used the physical move Close Combat,
your Will-O-Wisp inflicted them with a burn! A burned
Pokémon’s physical moves will have their damage halved,
and the Pokémon will also take damage at the end of the turn.

TextInfos.Text: After the opponent used the physical move Close Combat,
your Will-O-Wisp inflicted them with a
physical moves will have their damage halved
take damage at the end of the turn

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Let’s test for ourselves whether the opponent’s physical moves
really do only deal half as much damage as before, shall we?

This time, try choosing the move Flare Blitz.

TextInfos.Text: Let’s test for ourselves whether the opponent’s physical moves
really do only deal half as much damage as before, shall we?
This time, try choosing the move

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Not only did the opponent’s physical move, Close Combat,
have its damage halved, but the opponent also fainted due to
the damage it took from its burn!

Remember—inflicting status conditions on your opponents
will help you seize the upper hand in battles!

TextInfos.Text: Not only did the opponent’s physical move, Close Combat,

Remember—inflicting status conditions on your opponents
will help you seize the upper hand in battles!

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Contact vs. Noncontact Moves

Pokémon moves are broadly divided into two groups:
contact moves and noncontact moves. You can see
this categorization in the Move Info window that can
be displayed on the move selection screen.

TextInfos.Text: Pokémon moves are broadly divided into two groups:
be displayed on the move selection screen.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Let’s learn about the differences between contact moves
and noncontact moves by seeing how they interact with an
Ability that is triggered by contact moves.

Start by having Maushold here use Population Bomb.

TextInfos.Text: Let’s learn about the differences between contact moves
and noncontact moves by seeing how they interact with an
Ability that is triggered by contact moves.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Since Maushold came into contact with the opponent
upon using a contact move, it took damage from that
Pokémon’s Ability, Rough Skin.

And since Population Bomb is a multistrike move, Maushold
took damage each time it touched the opponent, adding up
to quite a lot of damage by the end of the turn.

TextInfos.Text: Since Maushold came into contact with the opponent
upon using a contact move, it took damage from that
And since Population Bomb is a multistrike move, Maushold
took damage each time it touched the opponent, adding up
to quite a lot of damage by the end of the turn.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Next, why don’t you see how things turn out when you
have Maushold use the noncontact move Bullet Seed?

TextInfos.Text: Next, why don’t you see how things turn out when you
have Maushold use the noncontact move

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Since that was a noncontact move, Maushold never
triggered its opponent’s Rough Skin Ability, meaning it
took no damage while attacking.

Remember that contact moves and noncontact moves
can have different impacts as you choose the right move
for each situation in battle!

TextInfos.Text: Since that was a noncontact move, Maushold never
triggered its opponent’s Rough Skin Ability, meaning it
Remember that contact moves and noncontact moves
can have different impacts as you choose the right move

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Weather Conditions

Weather conditions can have a big impact on a battle.
Let’s take a look at a scenario in which rain will help
turn the tide of battle in your favor!

Before we go changing the weather, though, it’s important
to understand where we’re starting from. Let’s begin by
having Basculegion use its Wave Crash move.

TextInfos.Text: can have a big impact on a battle.
Let’s take a look at a scenario in which
turn the tide of battle in your favor!
Before we go changing the weather, though, it’s important
to understand where we’re starting from. Let’s begin by

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Oof, the damage from that Wave Crash didn’t even
reduce the opposing Pokémon’s HP by half.

But what about if we try changing the weather?
Have Basculegion use its Rain Dance move next.

TextInfos.Text: Oof, the damage from that Wave Crash didn’t even
reduce the opposing Pokémon’s HP by half.
But what about if we try

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
The weather has changed to rain. When it’s raining,
the power of Water-type moves is boosted by 50%.

TextInfos.Text: the power of Water-type moves is boosted by 50%

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
But that’s not all. Basculegion’s Ability Swift Swim
also doubles its Speed stat when it’s raining.

Why don’t you try that Wave Crash again in this weather?

TextInfos.Text: But that’s not all. Basculegion’s Ability

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Thanks to the rain, that Water-type move had its power
boosted by 50% and Basculegion had its Speed stat doubled
by its Swift Swim Ability, meaning that it got to attack first!

Make good use of the weather to help you
turn the tides of battle in your favor!

TextInfos.Text: Thanks to the rain, that Water-type move had its power
boosted by 50% and Basculegion had its Speed stat doubled
by its Swift Swim Ability, meaning that it got to attack first!
Make good use of the weather to help you
turn the tides of battle in your favor!

mSDataSet.OriginalText: Terrains

Terrains can have various useful effects, including boosting
the power of certain types of moves.

Try having Hatterene here use the move Psychic Terrain.

TextInfos.Text: can have various useful effects, including boosting
the power of certain types of moves.

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Your opponent used the priority move Extreme Speed.

But priority moves fail if Psychic Terrain is active.

TextInfos.Text: Your opponent used the priority move

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
Why don’t you have your Pokémon try the move Psychic
now that Psychic Terrain is in effect?

TextInfos.Text: Why don’t you have your Pokémon try the move
now that Psychic Terrain is in effect?

mSDataSet.OriginalText:
The incoming Extreme Speed failed, and you also
managed to knock out the opponent with your attack!
This was helped by the fact that the power of Psychic-type
moves is boosted by 30% while Psychic Terrain is active.

Terrains have all kinds of effects that impact both your side
and your opponent’s side. Use them to your advantage!
